paragraphs.body: The Scorpion and the Frog

annotations.comment: Excited to read this one!

paragraphs.body: CONTENT WARNING: brief, nongraphic mentions of suicide on page 10
I met the poisonous girl in the spring of last year.

annotations.comment: Great first line. Give yourself some space between the content warning and that line.

paragraphs.body: You might think me harsh for such a descriptor, but she would agree with it. After all, she’s the one who used to call herself that. She warned me that every relationship she’s ever been in was tainted by her poison, and thereafter swiftly ended. I would just laugh whenever she said such things – isn’t that how every relationship ends? A little bit of poison will always seep in somehow – does it really matter where it comes from? “I suppose not,” she would say. But though she conceded, she would wrap her cardigan over her torso, crossing her arms over the fraying wool. She wouldn’t give me an inch.

annotations.comment: I like the voice here — a little heightened — and the narrator’s good description helps. I was a little confused by this sentence, because it feels like the girl did give an inch. (I also hope we’re going to find out her name soon.)

paragraphs.body: I had first seen the poisonous girl sleeping on a pile of garbage outside La Province, a high-caliber French restaurant I liked to frequent with the girls I dated. It was on a particularly cumbersome date with a drag of a girl that I decided I needed a cigarette. I chain smoked in the back alley, trying to pass the time, when I saw what appeared to be a thin white leg sticking out from the piles of trash bags strewn across the kitchen entrance. I almost jumped out of my skin, thinking it was a dead body. Cautiously, I crept up to the leg and noticed a black high heel teetering from the big toe. Upon closer inspection, the leg and the shoe belonged to a girl, face barbed by tangled wisps of hair pulled into a wild ponytail.

annotations.text: pile of garbage outside La Province
annotations.comment: I just didn’t buy this. Who sleeps on garbage?

paragraphs.body: “Um…Miss?” I asked, poking the leg with my foot. The girl jolted awake at once, sitting up with a sharp inhale. Her blouse was wrinkled and her black apron was on sideways, the thin drawstring resting on her hip.

annotations.comment: It’s interesting that the spoken voice of the narrator seems much more reticent than what we met above.

paragraphs.body: “What time is it?” She asked.
I checked my Rolex. “Just after 9 o’clock.”

annotations.comment: Nice detail.

paragraphs.body: “Shit!” She scrambled to her feet, hopping towards the kitchen door on one foot as she jammed her other into the shoe. Without another word, she slammed the door shut behind her, leaving me awestruck in the alleyway.

annotations.comment: Again, I don’t buy that she fell asleep on bags of garbage. Maybe out back. Maybe passed out near garbage. But…I also wanted to note that I wasn’t sure why this guy was awestruck by her.

paragraphs.body: I encountered the poisonous girl a few more times at La Province after our strange initial meeting. She was a busgirl there – full-time, supporting herself, no family to speak of. She wasn’t attending university; hell, she had barely managed to graduate high school. As for why she was sleeping by the dumpster that day, she told me she liked to kill time back there whenever her manager wasn’t paying attention. That day we met, she had accidentally fallen asleep and was severely chewed out for it.

annotations.comment: Maybe “I learned…”

paragraphs.body: This made me laugh – girls never behaved so brazenly anymore, especially when they thought no one would catch them acting that way. It was cute. The girl blushed when I said this, fiddling with her long braid.

annotations.comment: Great detail to tie back into the inspiration.

paragraphs.body: But the poisonous girl was much tougher to crack than she seemed. For the longest time she wouldn’t agree to date me, always handing out rejections that ranged from coy teases to stony silences. But her most common excuse was an insistence that the relationship would inevitably come to an end. And not just any end – an explosive, violent one.

annotations.comment: I’d like a better sense of why he’s attracted to her. Is she that beautiful? Is there some other quality he’s drawn to?

paragraphs.body: I always laughed her off, saying she was crazy. “How could you possibly know that before it even happens?” I asked. She shrugged. “Just a feeling,” she said.

She finally gave in by the end of summer. We went to a bar on a Saturday evening and took things back to my place. We sat on my couch, hiccupping and singing and laughing, though now I can’t remember what exactly had been so funny.

annotations.text: She finally gave in by the end of summer
annotations.comment: Maybe give us some idea as to why she gives in?
annotations.context_right: . We went to a bar o

paragraphs.body: All I remember is I stopped laughing and opened my eyes. Sitting there on my couch, in unfettered beauty, sat the poisonous girl. The rouge of drunkenness tinged every sharp corner on the girl’s body, from her nose to her cheekbones to the delicate marbling of shadow on her collarbones. She threw her head back, her unpredictable split ends curling every which way like auburn fireworks dancing around her shoulders. She squeezed her eyes so tight and beamed so brightly that crows’ feet winked at the outer corners of her eyes. In that moment, I could see us growing old together, loving each other forever.

annotations.comment: I’m having trouble tracking what her hair actually looks like. Is that deliberate?
I don’t think I quite realized that he had really fallen for her. Or maybe he hasn’t? Just not quite following what’s going on here.

paragraphs.body: I have to have her, I realized.
When she saw me staring, she stopped laughing. “What?” She asked, alarmed. “Is there something on my face?”
“Be with me,” I said, reaching out to cup her cheeks. “From this day on, I will adore you, and only you, until the day I die. Wherever you go, I will follow. I promise you, so please.”

annotations.comment: Give some thought to how you want the story to come off to readers. This language is so heightened, it’s hard to know exactly what he’s asking. It’s almost *too* fairy tale for the setting (a fairly normal American city, I think?) you’ve chosen. He sounds so over the top, so it’s hard to know how to take this interaction. I’m interested by how she responds, but not really clear on what just happened.

paragraphs.body: For once, the girl was silent. The earnestness of her gaze sent an exciting chill down my spine – it was as if she were reading me, penetrating my very soul to verify my truth and devotion. She gently plucked my hands from her face, leaning back. For a moment something changed in her expression, though it was so quick I could hardly see it happen. It was a softening, I think. She had seen me, and she had decided.
“Okay,” she said. “I’ll be with you.” It was so simple, and yet just like that, it came to be.

Life with the poisonous girl went on quite as it had before she became my girlfriend. It still remained a pretty casual affair – I had work in the daytime and she had bussing in the night – but though our schedules clashed, we made it work. Our devotion to each other was more than enough. We were just that good together.

annotations.text: till remained a pretty casual affair
annotations.comment: After all that, they still stay casual?

annotations.text: We were just that good together.
annotations.comment: I want to see a scene that lets me decide this for myself.

paragraphs.body: I guess things first began to change when she got evicted from her apartment.

It wasn’t because of anything she did, specifically. At least that’s what she told me. The night she came knocking on my door, eyes blood red and hair clumped at her scalp in pale-knuckled fistfuls, she paced back and forth in my living room trying to choke out an explanation between sobs. From what I gathered, her landlord sold her place to another tenant, giving her a two months’ notice to get out.

annotations.comment: I’m glad to see some problems beginning here. It’s a nice touch that she has two months to find a new place but is still overreacting anyway.
annotations.context_left: at her scalp in pale-knuckled fistfuls, she paced back and forth in my living room trying to choke
annotations.context_right: an explanation between sobs. From what I gathered, her landlord sold her place to another tenant, g

paragraphs.body: I held her close as the tears came gushing out, her muscles turning to jelly in my embrace. I felt pretty awkward, I have to admit – nothing like this had ever happened to me, after all. I stroked her hair and patted her back, desperate to think of something useful to say.

annotations.text: nothing like this had ever happened to me
annotations.comment: It’s interesting to use their class difference as coming between them.

paragraphs.body: “Why don’t you stay with me?”
When I said this, she gave me a watery, incredulous look. I shifted my feet. Those pervasive eyes sometimes made me feel so uncomfortable.
“No, I couldn’t,” she finally said, taking a step back to grip her arms, shrinking inward. “This is my problem, not yours. I’d really hate to impose like that.”

That’s fair, I thought. In all honesty, I had only really asked on an impulse – it just felt like the right thing to do. I’m very particular about my way of living; an unexpected guest was a factor I would’ve needed much more time to prepare for.
But when I looked at the girl – her hand darting to swipe a freshly fallen tear with one hand as she clutched the lining of her cardigan with the other – I knew I wouldn’t take no for an answer.

annotations.comment: It makes sense to me that he’d be ambivalent and maybe feel a little put upon about having to offer, so I would suggest that maybe he doesn’t make an intense declaration of love earlier?

paragraphs.body: So it came to be that the poisonous girl took residence on my pull-out couch.

annotations.comment: He has a Rolex and can only give her a pull-out couch? Wow.

paragraphs.body: “It’s only temporary, I swear. Just until I can find a new place,” she assured me over and over again. I swept her up into a kiss. “Stay as long as you need,” I said.

Things were going great for a while. We still had our own jobs and lives to attend to, after all, so we rarely crossed paths. Life was still beautiful with the poisonous girl, and I was sure things would only get better from there.

annotations.text: Things were going great for a while.
annotations.comment: You would help your story by spending this up a bit.

paragraphs.body: But my delusions were short-lived. I came home from work one day to find the girl sitting on the couch, numbly staring at the wall.

I almost jumped out of my skin – she looked like a ghost, all pale and sunken like that. It reminded me of the first time we had met, when she appeared like an apparition in the dump outside her workplace. This made me pause. Wait, why isn’t she at work? I had wondered. When I asked her about this, she barely moved her mouth to answer.
“I got fired today.”

annotations.comment: It’s a great technique to up the stakes here. I did wonder why this is so surprising to both of them. She seemed to be pretty bad at her job!

paragraphs.body: I stood there like an idiot in the doorway, unsure of what to say. No, it can’t be, it’s not possible!, I had thought. But despite my gut reaction, it slowly dawned on me that this was in fact not only possible, but probably long overdue. From what I’d seen of the poisonous girl in action – well, the gentle way of putting it is that she sucked at her job. She was undeniably charming and bright, though I had to admit she definitely wasn’t the most refined or tactful. I’m honestly surprised she was hired at all.

annotations.comment: Yeah, can he have that reaction right away?
She needs a name.

annotations.text: definitely wasn’t the most refined or tactful
annotations.comment: It’d be great to see his attitude towards her in the way he treats her a bit more.

paragraphs.body: For my loss of words, all I could do was walk up to the couch, plop down next to her, and put my hand on her shoulder. She didn’t even move at my touch, a storminess glancing between her eyes. It was a look of confusion, or devastation. Or both.

“You can bounce right back from this,” I assured her. I gave her a little shake. Suddenly, I perked up, remembering something. “Come to think of it, I have a spare laptop somewhere in my room – it’s half-broken, but it works well enough. If you start filling out job applications tomorrow, I’m sure you can be working again by next month. Hell, maybe even in a couple weeks!”

annotations.text: it’s half-broken, but it works well enough
annotations.comment: I think you’re on to something with how he kind of half-helps her. It shows his care but also his disdain. He obviously could afford to buy her a new laptop. But he doesn’t.

paragraphs.body: She turned her head, acknowledging me directly for the first time that evening. She slowly nodded, giving me a small smile. “Thanks,” she said. “You’re right. I’ll do it tomorrow.”

When I left her the next morning, she had regained some of her strength, though she was still understandably deflated. Before I went to work, I made sure she was all set with the computer. I showed her how to organize her notes and information into different portals and folders. I scrubbed the desktop clean, leaving her a blank canvas for the next chapter of her life. Computer humming warmly on her lap, she waved goodbye as I left for work. The last things I saw were her fingers poking out from raw-hemmed sweater paws as I shut the door.

annotations.comment: I’d like to see this scene too. How does she respond to this?

paragraphs.body: This is how I left her every workday morning for the next several days. Every time I came home, she would be sleeping on the couch, or else she’d be watching T.V., getting crumbs all over my freshly-vacuumed rug, or even just lying down, staring at the ceiling. “How did the jobhunting go today?” I would ask. “Get any interviews?”

annotations.comment: Who’s doing the vacuuming?

paragraphs.body: “Hmm, not today,” she would say, smiling sorrowfully in a way that said oh well, maybe tomorrow.
“No rush,” I would smile back.
[Cut for page length: the girl’s increasingly sloppy lifestyle really starts to agitate the narrator. Currently, she is out of the apartment, and he is sitting on the couch when he sees the laptop.]

annotations.comment: You can speed this up.

paragraphs.body: At that moment, a thought popped into my head. Even at that time, I knew I shouldn’t have done it. But as I sat there, curious and agitated, I felt a dangerous heat prickle up my spine. I couldn’t help myself. I snatched up the laptop.

As I scanned the screen, I couldn’t believe my eyes. There wasn’t a single tab open, nor a single new folder created. The desktop was squeaky clean. I checked the search history, emails, everything – I found nothing.

annotations.comment: Wow, I wonder what she does do all day.

paragraphs.body: It may as well have been a brand-new laptop.

The prickling heat in my chest boiled over into full on rage. I slammed the laptop onto the table. I ripped the cushions off the couch, throwing them at the wall. I spun around to kick in the stupid fucking T.V. she had worshipped; the hunk of plastic she seemed to love more than she loved me.

annotations.comment: This feels a little off — he hadn’t connected finding a job to loving him earlier. Did that happen at some point? Why is freaking out like this and destroying his own stuff?
annotations.context_left: The prickling heat in my chest boiled over into full on rage. I slam
annotations.context_right: d the laptop onto the table. I ripped the cushions off the couch, throwing them at the wall. I spun

paragraphs.body: I didn’t even hear the poisonous girl come in. I don’t know how much of it she saw, but I don’t think it matters much, anyway. The sizable fracture in the television, the shards of plastic and glass littering the ground, the laptop laying on my scarred coffee table in two separate halves. The scene of the crime spoke for itself.

When our eyes met, she brought a trembling hand to her mouth. Somehow, the unconcealed horror on her face made me even angrier – what right did she have to look at me that way? The one who had brought her back to stable ground when her life had fallen to pieces?

annotations.text: stable ground when her life had
annotations.comment: I’d like to see more of this expectation from him earlier. Let us see him assuring her he’s going to save her.

paragraphs.body: I stormed over to the girl and gripped her arms. She gasped as I shook her, forcing her to look me in the eye.
“I remembered, while you were away,” I gritted out. “You told me before that you weren’t in contact with anyone in your family.” Her eyes widened. She was caught.

annotations.comment: Hard to respond to this since we didn’t read that section.

paragraphs.body: “So who did you really go to see?” I pressed. “Spending my money with your stupid friends? Or going off to meet another boyfriend, maybe?”
She started to cry. I just watched her, waiting for an answer.

“Okay, I’ll be honest with you,” she said after a while. “I’ll tell you, so will you please let go?” I immediately released my grip on her arms – I hadn’t even realized I was still holding them. She gingerly cradled the reddened spots on her flesh like they were diseased, burning pockmarks. She bent her head low. The way her hair fell limply across her face reminded me of a corpse swaying on the gallows.

annotations.comment: I’m just noting that it’s hard to like the girl as you’ve written her. I think that’s okay given his disdain for her, but just pointing it out.

paragraphs.body: “The truth is,” she continued. “I lied about it all. I don’t leave the house, and I haven’t been looking for work. The only thing I haven’t lied about is the river. I go on walks sometimes. That’s the only time I go outside.” She looked into my eyes. They betrayed nothing, so she continued.

annotations.comment: So, what’s her deal? Depression?

paragraphs.body: “Lately, I’ve just been in a sort of funk. I don’t know if that’s the right word, but...” She trailed off. I could tell she was trying to be strong, but by the way her voice quivered and her eyes darted everywhere but mine, it must’ve been hard for her to talk about.
Still, I did not accept her answer.
“I don’t understand this,” I said, rubbing my forehead. “You say you’ve been – what, unwell? When I met you just a few months ago, you seemed perfectly fine.”

annotations.comment: She was asleep in garbage though?

paragraphs.body: The girl narrowed her eyes. “What exactly are you implying?” She hissed.

annotations.comment: I don’t quite buy the dialogue, sorry. I can’t tell if she’s lying or what.

paragraphs.body: “All I’m saying is that it definitely looks a certain way when these feelings seem to crop up as soon as you’re living a cushy, unemployed life on my couch,” I said. “Are you sure it’s not just a mindset – that you haven’t decided to let yourself go?”

She looked at me, clearly hurt. “I’m telling the truth!” She exclaimed. “I’ve been having a really, really hard time. These dark thoughts – they come and go. When you met me, I was managing them fine. But ever since we started dating, it’s like they’ve been getting worse again.”
“Are you saying this is my fault?” I shouted. She pursed her lips.

“All I’m saying,” she said carefully, “is that I’ve been having a hard time. It doesn’t help that you’re constantly asking me about jobs and applications when I find it hard enough to wake up and get off the couch every morning as it is.”
She paused to collect herself; I could tell she was on the verge of tears. She took a deep, shaky breath, then continued.
“Honestly, there are things I probably could’ve done differently. I could have been honest with you, for one.” She tightened the grip on her arms, looking to the side.

annotations.comment: This dialogue is good but can be reworked a bit — maybe he say she could have been more honest and she says no, she could not have?
annotations.context_right: stly, there are things I probably could’ve done differently. I could have been honest with you, for

paragraphs.body: “Actually, no.” She breathed, almost to herself. “I don’t think I could have been honest with you. I knew you’d react like this.”

I felt the swell of rage reignite within my chest. “Well, if I’m such a bad guy and I only add more baggage to your little basket case situation, then why don’t we just end it here?” I stood up, glaring down at her. “That includes you moving out,” I added.
She knit her eyebrows together, her big eyes flickering at me. “Right now?” She asked. “You know I have nowhere else to go – I don’t have a job, or money, or anyone else to rely on.”

annotations.comment: The stakes feel a little unrealistic — she can’t get a hotel room or something?

paragraphs.body: I checked my Rolex impassively. “Not my problem. Maybe if you had taken my advice and gotten a job a month ago, you wouldn’t be in this situation now.”
She dropped her arms, staring at me in that way. That sickening, soul-baring way that made me feel so damn small and transparent. It terrified me.

annotations.comment: I don’t really know what this looks like.

paragraphs.body: “I feel like you didn’t hear anything I just said,” she said lowly. “But I’ll tell you this, one last time – I’m asking for your help right now. Is this really your answer?”

I looked away uncomfortably. “I’ve helped you enough,” I said. I walked to the door, and though I never looked back, I could feel her eyes watching every sliver of me, tracing every single movement. The feeling was maddening – I wanted to rip my skin off, scream, do something, anything. But I just opened the door, cocking my head ever so slightly.
“I’ve got some work to do. As soon as you pack your bags, you can show yourself out. Leave my key in the mailbox.” And with that, I closed the door.

I slept at the office that night. I’m not usually a cowardly person, but I was somehow too afraid to return to my apartment to see if the poisonous girl was still there. In the early afternoon of the following day, I finally worked up the courage to go back.

As I approached my building, I was surprised to see a couple of police officers outside the premises. When they spotted me, their demeanors shifted and they stood up to meet me. A cold feeling suddenly washed over me, and I immediately knew.

The interrogation process was quicker than I thought it’d be; wrapped up within the day, in fact. They simply had to ask a few compulsory questions since I was the last known person she had been in contact with, though it was pretty much an open and shut case.

annotations.text: she had been in contact with
annotations.comment: I guess I wondered how they knew she was staying with him.

paragraphs.body: The poisonous girl was found in the river. She had gone and drowned herself. It was the same river she loved to walk along, though I can’t tell you which part that was exactly. I don’t know it.

It hasn’t been that long, but it seems like everybody has already forgotten about her. Or rather, it’s like she never even existed in the first place. Life goes on, I guess. As I’ve said, she had no family. She had no job. I was all she had.

It’s almost spring again. As time passes, I find that the bad things melt away – they become blurry and foreign like a bad dream I can’t quite make out. I only remember, with burning vivacity, the good times we shared together. And I miss her. I’ve taken to walking along the river these days. As I walk, I wonder if this is the route she liked to take. I pause to lean on the railing. I stare into the water. I will keep my promise.

annotations.comment: It’s hard to feel sorry for this guy. So, Camila, congratulations on a first draft! It’s clear you worked really hard on this and I enjoyed reading it. I was worried when you said in class that you hadn’t thought much about the time period and setting of your stories, and what I feared has come to pass: it’s hard to locate this story in time and place. You wrote a heightened narrator in a sort of faux fairy-tale style, but there are elements that locate the story in our time (or nearly so, anyway). The characters speak so generally to each other, though, that it’s hard to really picture them. And the events of the story are a bit difficult to believe as well. So at times it seems like you do want to write more of a fable, but then at other points, it’s more of a realistic story. I’d give some thought to that. Since it already is a fable, I think I’d try writing a realistic story — I’d like to see these two have a real conversation, to try to understand the effect and hold they have on each other. Is she manipulating him? Is he enjoying being the Sugar Daddy? What’s going on there? I also didn’t quite see the connection to the Scorpion and the Frog. I think the point of that story is that some people can’t help but cause their own downfall, even when they know better. But I didn’t really see that here — a sometimes cruel guy sort of helps a young woman with some severe mental health issues, until he gets tired of her, and then she dies by suicide, which seemingly inspires him to do the same. How does that tie back to the inspiration? Now, to be sure, you can say, nevermind, it’s just a story, no connection, and that’s fine. But then it really doesn’t make sense as a sort of fable, you know? And I liked the idea (and I think you did too) of a riff on the original — I just think you need to think through more clearly, how to do that. It’s a nice first draft, Audrey — interested to see where you go with it. Happy writing!
